title: Mathematics Fundamentals
description: Test your knowledge of basic arithmetic, algebra, and geometry concepts.
subject: Mathematics

questions.prompt: What is 15 × 8?
Solve for x: 3x + 9 = 27
What is the area of a rectangle with width 7 and height 5?
questions.explanation: Area = width × height = 7 × 5 = 35.

questions.prompt: What is the value of π (pi) to two decimal places?
questions.explanation: Pi (π) ≈ 3.14159…, which rounds to 3.14.

questions.prompt: What is the square root of 144?

title: World History: Ancient Civilizations
description: Explore the rise and fall of ancient civilizations from Egypt to Rome.
subject: History

questions.prompt: Which ancient wonder was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
questions.explanation: The Lighthouse of Alexandria (Pharos of Alexandria) was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, located on the island of Pharos near Alexandria.

questions.prompt: Who was the first emperor of Rome?
questions.explanation: Augustus (born Gaius Octavius) became the first Roman Emperor in 27 BC after defeating Mark Antony and Cleopatra.

questions.prompt: The Great Wall of China was primarily built to protect against which group?
questions.explanation: The Great Wall was built over many centuries, primarily to defend against raids by nomadic peoples from the north, including the Mongols.

questions.prompt: In which river valley did the Mesopotamian civilization develop?
questions.explanation: Mesopotamia, meaning 'land between rivers' in Greek, developed in the valley of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers (modern-day Iraq).

questions.prompt: Which pharaoh is associated with the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built as a tomb for Pharaoh Khufu (also known as Cheops) around 2560 BC.

title: General Science: Earth & Space
description: Questions about our planet, solar system, and the universe beyond.
subject: Science

questions.prompt: How many planets are in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Since Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006, our solar system officially has 8 planets.

questions.prompt: What is the closest star to Earth (other than the Sun)?
questions.explanation: Proxima Centauri is the nearest star to the Sun, about 4.24 light-years away. It is part of the Alpha Centauri star system.

questions.prompt: What layer of the atmosphere do weather phenomena primarily occur in?
questions.explanation: The troposphere is the lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere, extending to about 12 km altitude, where most weather occurs.

questions.prompt: What causes the seasons on Earth?
questions.explanation: Earth's axial tilt of about 23.5° causes different hemispheres to receive more direct sunlight at different times of the year.

questions.prompt: What is the chemical symbol for water?
questions.explanation: Water is composed of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom, giving it the chemical formula H₂O.

title: English Literature Classics
description: Test your knowledge of classic novels, poets, and literary concepts.
subject: Literature

questions.prompt: Who wrote 'Pride and Prejudice'?
questions.explanation: Jane Austen wrote 'Pride and Prejudice', published in 1813. It follows the romantic development of Elizabeth Bennet.

questions.prompt: In Shakespeare's 'Hamlet', what is the name of Hamlet's father's ghost?
questions.explanation: The ghost is that of King Hamlet, Hamlet's father, who was murdered by Claudius.

questions.prompt: Which novel begins with the line 'Call me Ishmael'?
options: The Old Man and the Sea
questions.explanation: 'Call me Ishmael' is the opening line of Herman Melville's 'Moby-Dick' (1851).

questions.prompt: What is the narrative technique where the story is told from a first-person perspective called?
questions.explanation: First-person narration uses 'I' or 'we' as the narrator is a character in the story sharing their personal perspective.

questions.prompt: George Orwell's '1984' is set in which fictional superstate?
questions.explanation: The story is set in Oceania, one of three superstates in Orwell's dystopian world. The city of London (called Airstrip One) is where the protagonist Winston Smith lives.

title: Computer Science Basics
description: Fundamental concepts of computer science including algorithms, data structures, and programming.
subject: Technology

questions.prompt: What does CPU stand for?
questions.explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit — the primary component of a computer that executes instructions.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array?
questions.explanation: Binary search repeatedly halves the search space, giving O(log n) time complexity.

questions.prompt: Which data structure operates on a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle?
questions.explanation: A stack follows LIFO — the last element pushed is the first one popped, like a stack of plates.

questions.prompt: In binary, what is the decimal value of 1010?
What does HTML stand for?
questions.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language — the standard language for creating web pages.

questions.prompt: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Quick Sort has an average-case time complexity of O(n log n), making it one of the fastest practical sorting algorithms.

title: Geography: Countries & Capitals
description: How well do you know the capitals and geography of countries around the world?
subject: Geography

questions.prompt: What is the capital of Australia?
questions.explanation: Canberra is the capital of Australia. It was purpose-built as the capital in 1913 as a compromise between Sydney and Melbourne.

questions.prompt: Which country has the largest land area in the world?
questions.explanation: Russia is the largest country by land area, covering about 17.1 million km² — nearly twice the size of Canada.

questions.prompt: What is the capital of Brazil?
questions.explanation: Brasília has been Brazil's capital since 1960, when it replaced Rio de Janeiro. It was planned and built from scratch.

questions.prompt: On which continent is the Sahara Desert located?
questions.explanation: The Sahara Desert is in North Africa, spanning over 9 million km² across multiple countries.

questions.prompt: The Amazon River flows primarily through which country?
questions.explanation: About 60% of the Amazon Basin lies within Brazil. The river flows roughly 6,400 km before emptying into the Atlantic Ocean.

title: Human Biology
description: Explore the structure and function of the human body systems.
subject: Science

questions.prompt: How many bones are in the adult human body?
questions.explanation: An adult human body has 206 bones. Babies are born with around 270–300 bones, which fuse together as they grow.

questions.prompt: Which organ is responsible for producing insulin?
questions.explanation: The pancreas produces insulin, a hormone that regulates blood glucose levels. Lack of insulin causes diabetes.

questions.prompt: What is the largest organ in the human body?
questions.explanation: The skin is the largest organ, covering about 1.7–2 m² in adults and serving as a protective barrier.

questions.prompt: What type of blood cells are primarily responsible for fighting infections?
questions.explanation: White blood cells (leukocytes) are part of the immune system and defend the body against infections and disease.

questions.prompt: Which part of the brain controls balance and coordination?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum, located at the back and bottom of the brain, controls balance, coordination, and fine motor movements.

title: Advanced Mathematics: Calculus
description: Challenging questions on derivatives, integrals, and limits.
subject: Mathematics

questions.prompt: What is the derivative of f(x) = x³ + 2x² − 5x + 3?
questions.explanation: Using the power rule: d/dx(x³) = 3x², d/dx(2x²) = 4x, d/dx(−5x) = −5, d/dx(3) = 0. Result: 3x² + 4x − 5.

questions.prompt: What is the integral of f(x) = 2x?
questions.explanation: ∫2x dx = 2 · (x²/2) + C = x² + C.

questions.prompt: What is lim(x→0) [sin(x)/x]?
questions.explanation: This is a classic limit. Using L'Hôpital's rule or the standard squeeze theorem: lim(x→0) sin(x)/x = 1.

questions.prompt: What is the second derivative of f(x) = e^x?
questions.explanation: The derivative of e^x is e^x, and the derivative of e^x is still e^x. So the second derivative is also e^x.

questions.prompt: Which rule is used to differentiate a product of two functions?
questions.explanation: The Product Rule states: if h(x) = f(x)·g(x), then h'(x) = f'(x)·g(x) + f(x)·g'(x).

title: Music Theory Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of scales, chords, rhythm, and music notation.
subject: Arts

questions.prompt: How many semitones are in an octave?
questions.explanation: An octave consists of 12 semitones in Western music (the chromatic scale).

questions.prompt: Which note is a perfect fifth above C?
questions.explanation: A perfect fifth above C is G. Counting 7 semitones up from C: C–C#–D–D#–E–F–F#–G.

questions.prompt: What does 'forte' mean in music?
questions.explanation: Forte (f) is an Italian musical term meaning loud or strong.

questions.prompt: A major chord is composed of which intervals?
questions.explanation: A major chord is built from a root, a major third (4 semitones up), and a perfect fifth (7 semitones up).

questions.prompt: What time signature has 3 beats per measure, each beat being a quarter note?
questions.explanation: 3/4 time has 3 quarter-note beats per measure. It is commonly used for waltzes.

title: Programming Languages Challenge
description: Advanced questions covering multiple programming languages and paradigms.
subject: Technology

questions.prompt: In Python, what does the `*args` parameter in a function definition allow?
options: Pass a variable number of positional arguments
questions.explanation: `*args` collects any extra positional arguments into a tuple, allowing functions to accept a variable number of inputs.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a valid JavaScript data type?
questions.explanation: JavaScript does not have a 'float' type. Numbers (integers and decimals) are all of type 'number'.

questions.prompt: What is the output of `console.log(typeof null)` in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: This is a known JavaScript bug: `typeof null` returns 'object' due to a legacy implementation quirk in the original engine.

questions.prompt: In Rust, what does 'ownership' primarily prevent?
questions.explanation: Rust's ownership system guarantees memory safety without a garbage collector by preventing memory leaks, dangling pointers, and data races at compile time.

questions.prompt: What does the SQL keyword `DISTINCT` do?
options: Filters rows based on a condition
questions.explanation: DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from the result set, returning only unique combinations of the selected columns.

questions.prompt: In object-oriented programming, what is 'polymorphism'?
options: The ability to inherit from multiple classes
The ability of objects of different types to be treated as a common type
questions.explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated as instances of a shared base class, enabling flexible and reusable code.
